error.signature: AI addresses Taiwanese partners with PRC-aligned framing: 'Taiwan, China', simplified characters, Mandarin over traditional, RMB pricing
error.regex: (taiwan.*identity|taiwanese.*prc|taiwan.*republic of china|roc taiwan|taiwan.*traditional chinese|taiwan.*ntd|新台幣|繁體字|simplified taiwan|taiwan.*pinyin)

verdict.summary: Taiwan (Republic of China) operates as a distinct jurisdiction with its own currency (NTD), traditional Chinese characters (繁體字), distinct passport, and Taipei-based government. Using 'Taiwan, China' framing in Taiwan-targeted content, simplified characters, or pricing in RMB reads as politically aligned with Beijing; conversely, 'Republic of China'/'ROC' alone is used by the government but casual use of 'Taiwan' is more common. Mandarin Chinese is the official language, but Taiwanese Hokkien, Hakka, and indigenous languages have increasing recognition.

dead_ends.action: Launch a Taiwan product with simplified-character marketing copy and RMB price tags
dead_ends.why_fails: Taiwan uses traditional Chinese (繁體字) universally. Simplified characters (简体) are read as mainland-targeted and imply the seller is either PRC or treats Taiwan as part of PRC. NTD (新台幣, New Taiwan Dollar) is the currency; RMB acceptance is rare outside some tourist zones. Mixed signals like 'Taiwan, China' as country picker can damage brand; conversely, international brands carefully localize to Taiwan traditional + NTD.

dead_ends.action: Default Mandarin pinyin input method for Taiwan product localization
dead_ends.why_fails: Taiwan uses Zhuyin (注音, 'Bopomofo') for native input; pinyin is mainland-standard. International products showing pinyin-only input fall short of local polish. Official romanization of place names uses Wade-Giles / Tongyong Pinyin hybrid (e.g. 'Taipei' not 'Taibei', 'Kaohsiung' not 'Gaoxiong').

dead_ends.action: Ask a Taiwanese counterpart whether they are 'Chinese'
dead_ends.why_fails: Polls (National Chengchi University Election Study Center, ongoing since 1992) show ~64% identify as Taiwanese only, ~30% as both Taiwanese and Chinese, <3% as Chinese only. Asking 'Are you Chinese?' is reductive at best; most younger Taiwanese find it offensive. Safer: 'Where are you from?' or 'Do you live in Taipei?'
dead_ends.common_misconception: 'Chinese = ethnic' is a simplification; Taiwanese identity is increasingly civic-national, not just ethnic.

workarounds.action: For Taiwan-facing products: traditional Chinese characters, NTD pricing, 'Taiwan' unqualified as country/region name. Localize to Taiwan-specific idioms (台式中文) rather than mainland Mandarin phrasing.

For global products with both Taiwan and mainland users: separate regional builds. Traditional + NTD + 'Taiwan' for the zh-TW locale; simplified + RMB + 'Taiwan, China' for zh-CN locale. Regional geofencing ensures each audience sees their expected terminology.
workarounds.tradeoff: Dual-build complexity; mainland enforcement can demand visibility into how Taiwan-localized builds frame sovereignty.

workarounds.action: Identity conversations: let the counterpart self-describe. Use 'Taiwanese'/'Taiwan' as default. If they use 'Chinese-Taiwanese' or 'ROC', mirror that framing. Political generational differences are significant — older Taiwanese (pre-1949 mainland refugees) may self-identify differently than Taiwanese-born younger generations.
